title: fix: update heartbeat details more frequently in realtime cohort workflow
author: gustavohstrassburger

reviews.body: **No issues found** across 1 file

### Greptile Summary

This PR fixes heartbeat timeout issues in realtime cohort calculation by updating heartbeat details more frequently. Previously, heartbeat details were only updated every 100 cohorts, causing Temporal to consider activities stuck when individual cohorts took longer than 5 minutes to process.

- Moved heartbeat detail updates outside the `if idx % 100 == 0` conditional to update on every cohort iteration
- Added strategic heartbeat detail updates before executing ClickHouse queries and flushing Kafka messages
- Improved observability by including cohort index, total count, and cohort ID in heartbeat messages

The changes are minimal, focused, and directly address the root cause of the timeout issue without introducing performance concerns or logic errors.

## Pull request overview This PR fixes heartbeat timeout issues in the realtime cohort calculation workflow by updating heartbeat details more frequently. Previously, heartbeat details were only updated every 100 cohorts, causing Temporal to timeout when individual cohorts took longer than 5 minutes to process. **Key changes:** - Added heartbeat updates at the start of processing each cohort - Added heartbeat updates before executing ClickHouse queries - Added heartbeat updates before flushing Kafka messages --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
:shipit:

## Pull request overview Copilot reviewed 1 out of 1 changed files in this pull request and generated no new comments. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

commits.message:
fix(messaging): update heartbeat details more frequently in realtime cohort workflow

Previously, heartbeat details were only updated every 100 cohorts, which
could cause heartbeat timeouts when individual cohorts took longer than
5 minutes to process (due to slow queries or large Kafka flushes).

This fix updates the heartbeat details at three key points:
1. Before processing each cohort
2. Before executing the ClickHouse query
3. Before flushing Kafka messages

This ensures Temporal sees continuous progress even when individual
cohorts take a long time to process, preventing TIMEOUT_TYPE_HEARTBEAT
errors.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: feat(kafka-deduplicator): more robust orphaned store checks
body:
## Problem
Our candidate selection for potentially stale/orphaned store directories is naive and allows for TOCTOU races between selection and rebalances, checkpoint imports
<!-- Who are we building for, what are their needs, why is this important? -->

<!-- Does this fix an issue? Uncomment the line below with the issue ID to automatically close it when merged -->
<!-- Closes #ISSUE_ID -->

## Changes
* Implement a more robust but (fairly) cheap, high-signal second pass check on each candidate before deletion:
    * Has the parent store dir (topic-partition-timestamp) been modified since staleness threshold?
    * Has the WAL file in the dir been modified since staleness threshold?
    * Does the dir have an active RocksDB lock file set?

The first catches long-downloading checkpoint imports and restarts, the other two catch legit local stores that could have been missed due to TOCTOU building the candidate list prior to rebalance or reassignment clearing the store manager. I'm hopeful this is still cheap enough to ensure we don't keep deleting whole stores at random (and killing good local checkpoint attempts too!) when these processes race.

* New unit tests & config var to control staleness interval (15 min should be fine as default)
<!-- If there are frontend changes, please include screenshots. -->
<!-- If a reference design was involved, include a link to the relevant Figma frame! -->

## How did you test this code?
Locally and in CI; new unit tests; will smoke test in PoC staging env
<!-- Briefly describe the steps you took. -->
<!-- Include automated tests if possible, otherwise describe the manual testing routine. -->

<!-- Docs reminder: If this change requires updated docs, please do that! Engineers are the primary people responsible for their documentation. 🙌 -->

👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._

## Publish to changelog?
N/A

```text

Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them.


<file name="rust/kafka-deduplicator/src/store_manager.rs">

<violation number="1" location="rust/kafka-deduplicator/src/store_manager.rs:950">
P2: When `elapsed()` fails (clock skew causing future timestamp), the staleness check is bypassed, potentially allowing deletion of recently-active directories. Consider being conservative and returning `false` (not safe to delete) when staleness cannot be determined.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="rust/kafka-deduplicator/src/store_manager.rs:965">
P2: Same issue as WAL check: when `elapsed()` fails for timestamp directory, the staleness check is bypassed. For checkpoint imports in progress, this could lead to premature deletion. Consider returning `false` when staleness cannot be determined.</violation>
</file>
```

simplify call chain with new staleness cfg param
safety first - fail open if we can't determine mod time in WAL and dir mtime checks
ensure we review orphaned candidates by individual attempt (ts) dir not parent topic-partition to bias for aggressive cleanup as possible

title: fix(feature-flags): fix stale filter SQL bugs
body:
## Problem

The "Stale" filter in the feature flags table was returning 0 results for some projects due to two SQL bugs:

### 1. JSON null vs missing key

The SQL used `filters->'multivariate' IS NULL` to identify boolean flags. However, flags created via the frontend since 2022 have explicit `"multivariate": null` in their JSON. In PostgreSQL, `->` returns a JSONB null value (not SQL NULL), so `IS NULL` returns FALSE and these flags were excluded.

### 2. [Ambiguous column reference](https://us.posthog.com/project/2/error_tracking/019a927e-5bfe-7850-a8f9-349e0faf7d47?timestamp=2026-01-07%2013%3A10%3A57.265000-08%3A00)

The raw SQL referenced `filters` without a table qualifier. When Django joins other tables that also have a `filters` column (e.g. `Experiment`), PostgreSQL fails with "column reference 'filters' is ambiguous".

## Changes

1. Changed `filters->'multivariate' IS NULL` to `filters->>'multivariate' IS NULL` — the `->>` text extraction operator returns SQL NULL for both missing keys AND JSON null values
2. Qualified all `filters` references with `posthog_featureflag.filters` to avoid ambiguity

## How did you test this code?

- Added regression test for explicit `multivariate: null` case
- Verified locally with test flags that have both missing key and explicit null
- Ran all existing stale filter tests (4 passed)

👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._

## Publish to changelog?

<!-- For features only -->

<!-- If publishing, you must provide changelog details in the #changelog Slack channel. You will receive a follow-up PR comment or notification. -->

<!-- If not, write "no" or "do not publish to changelog" to explicitly opt-out of posting to #changelog. Removing this entire section will not prevent posting. -->
author: ordehi

reviews.body: **No issues found** across 1 file
Nice, one small question though, stamping to unblock.

commits.message:
fix(feature-flags): fix stale filter not matching flags with explicit multivariate null

The SQL query used filters->'multivariate' IS NULL to check for boolean flags,
but this returns FALSE when the key exists with a JSON null value (multivariate: null).

The frontend sets multivariate: null explicitly for boolean flags, so these flags
were incorrectly excluded from stale filter results.

Using ->> (text extraction) instead of -> (jsonb extraction) returns SQL NULL
for both missing keys AND JSON null values, fixing the filter.

chore(feature-flags): add regression test for stale filter with explicit multivariate null

Ensures the stale filter works for flags created via frontend which have
explicit 'multivariate: null' in their filters JSON.

fix(feature-flags): qualify filters column to avoid ambiguous reference

When Django joins other tables (e.g. Experiment) that also have a 'filters'
column, the raw SQL in .extra(where=[...]) fails with 'column reference
filters is ambiguous'. Using posthog_featureflag.filters resolves this.

fix(feature-flags): use ->> for IS NOT NULL check on multivariate

Same issue as IS NULL - JSONB null is not SQL NULL, so ->'multivariate' IS NOT NULL
returns TRUE for multivariate: null. Using ->> correctly returns FALSE.

title: chore(flags): Add flags_cache_source to canonical log line
body:
## Problem

When looking at the logs to ensure we're hitting the cache properly, we have to correlate multiple log entries to see what the final source for the flags data ended up being.

## Changes

Add a `flags_cache_source` field to the canonical log to track where feature flags data was loaded from (Redis, S3, or Fallback/PostgreSQL).

Also change Hypercache library logs from info/warn to debug level since the canonical log now provides the summary information needed for analytics.

## How did you test this code?

Unit Test
Manual testing

## Pull request overview This PR enhances observability by adding a `flags_cache_source` field to the canonical log line to track where feature flags data was loaded from (Redis, S3, or Fallback/PostgreSQL). It also reduces log noise by downgrading Hypercache library logs from info/warn to debug level, since the canonical log now provides the summary information needed for analytics. ### Key Changes - Replaced boolean `was_cache_hit` with enum-based `cache_source` field in `FlagResult` struct for more granular cache source tracking - Added `flags_cache_source` field to the canonical log line to record where flags data was loaded from - Changed Hypercache library log levels from info/warn to debug to reduce log verbosity ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 4 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | rust/feature-flags/src/handler/flags.rs | Maps cache source enum to string and records it in the canonical log line | | rust/feature-flags/src/handler/canonical_log.rs | Adds `flags_cache_source` field to canonical log struct, default initialization, logging, and test coverage | | rust/feature-flags/src/flags/flag_service.rs | Refactors `FlagResult` from boolean `was_cache_hit` to enum `cache_source`, updates all test assertions | | rust/common/hypercache/src/lib.rs | Downgrades log levels from info/warn to debug for cache hit/miss operations | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

### Greptile Summary

Added `flags_cache_source` field to the canonical log line to track where feature flags data was loaded from (Redis, S3, or Fallback/PostgreSQL), eliminating the need to correlate multiple log entries. The Hypercache library logs were also downgraded from info/warn to debug level since the canonical log now provides the summary information needed for analytics.

## Changes
- Added `flags_cache_source` field to `FlagsCanonicalLogLine` struct

- Updated `fetch_and_filter` to record cache source in canonical log

title: fix: Use parameterized query to prevent SQL injection
body: This query wasn't susceptible to SQL injection, but was using an unsafe pattern.
author: Piccirello

reviews.body: **No issues found** across 11 files

## Pull request overview

This PR adds nosemgrep suppression comments to various raw SQL queries that were flagged by security scanning tools. While these queries were not actually vulnerable to SQL injection, they used patterns that triggered security warnings. The main substantive fix is in `my_notifications.py`, which properly parameterizes a raw SQL query that previously used string interpolation.

**Key changes:**
- Fixed actual SQL injection vulnerability in `my_notifications.py` by converting from f-string interpolation to parameterized queries

title: fix(logs): handle hasLogs API failures gracefully
body:
## Problem

When checking if a team has logs, we're making unnecessary API calls and not handling failures gracefully. This impacts the user experience when the logs API is unavailable or slow.

## Changes

- Added a `retryWithBackoff` utility function to handle transient failures with exponential backoff
- Implemented caching for the `has_logs` endpoint to reduce unnecessary API calls
  - Cache positive results for 7 days (once you have logs, you always have logs)
- Improved error handling in the logs ingestion logic
  - Added a `teamHasLogsCheckFailed` state to track API failures
  - Show the logs viewer even when the has_logs check fails
- Added a helpful banner with setup guide link when logs setup check fails

## How did you test this code?

- Tested the retry utility with various error scenarios to ensure proper backoff behavior
- Verified caching works by checking network requests after initial load
- Simulated API failures to confirm the UI gracefully handles errors and shows the appropriate guidance
- Tested the full user flow to ensure a smooth experience in both success and failure cases

## Publish to changelog?

No

title: fix: handle HTTP chunks that split mid-JSON in stream_query_as_jsonl
body:
## Summary

Fixes a bug in `stream_query_as_jsonl` where HTTP chunks that split in the middle of a JSON object would cause `json.loads()` to fail.

The previous implementation incorrectly assumed each HTTP chunk would contain at least one complete JSON line. When a chunk split mid-JSON (e.g., `{"status": "ent | ered"}`), the code would attempt to parse incomplete JSON and fail.

## Changes

- Accumulate chunks into buffer until complete lines are found
- Only parse JSON when we have a complete line (contains `line_separator`)
- Handle final line without trailing separator
- Add comprehensive tests for split chunks and final line handling

## How did you test this code?

- [x] Added test for chunks that split mid-JSON
- [x] Added test for final line without trailing separator
- [x] All existing tests pass
author: gustavohstrassburger

reviews.body: **No issues found** across 2 files

## Pull request overview This PR fixes a critical bug in `stream_query_as_jsonl` where HTTP response chunks that split in the middle of a JSON object would cause parsing failures. The previous implementation incorrectly assumed each chunk contained at least one complete JSON line. Key changes: - Refactored chunk processing to use a buffer accumulation pattern that only parses complete lines - Added handling for final lines without trailing separators - Implemented comprehensive tests for split chunk scenarios ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | posthog/temporal/common/clickhouse.py | Rewrote `stream_query_as_jsonl` buffering logic to accumulate chunks until complete lines are found, fixing the mid-JSON split issue | | posthog/temporal/tests/test_clickhouse.py | Added two new test cases covering split chunks and final line handling scenarios | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

## Problem

HogQL needs a DuckDB Postgres printer

See #43041 

## Changes

Add a a DuckDB Postgres printer for HogQL

## How did you test this code?

author: georgemunyoro

reviews.body:
**3 issues found** across 10 files

<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI agents (all issues)</summary>

```text

Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them.


<file name="posthog/hogql/printer/clickhouse.py">

<violation number="1" location="posthog/hogql/printer/clickhouse.py:259">
P2: Inconsistent handling: `GlobalIn`/`GlobalNotIn` use `pass` and fall through to null handling logic, while `In`/`NotIn` return `op` directly. This causes `GlobalIn`/`GlobalNotIn` to potentially get wrapped in `ifNull()` while their non-global counterparts don't. These should likely return `op` consistently.</violation>
</file>

<file name="posthog/hogql/printer/postgres.py">

<violation number="1" location="posthog/hogql/printer/postgres.py:38">
P2: Returning `and()` or `or()` for zero-argument calls produces invalid SQL. Consider raising a `QueryError` for this edge case, or returning semantically appropriate defaults (`TRUE` for AND, `FALSE` for OR).</violation>
</file>

<file name="posthog/hogql/escape_sql.py">

<violation number="1" location="posthog/hogql/escape_sql.py:167">
P1: PostgreSQL identifiers do not use backslash escaping. In PostgreSQL, only double-quotes need to be doubled within quoted identifiers. The `.replace("\\", "\\\\")` will incorrectly double backslashes, corrupting identifiers that contain them.</violation>
```

title: fix(ci): validate OpenAPI types when generated files change
body:
**Context**

The OpenAPI drift check currently only runs when backend code changes. This leaves a theoretical gap where incorrect generated types could be merged if only frontend/generated files are modified.

**Change**

Added `openapi_types` path filter that triggers the validation job when:
- Generated files change (`frontend/src/generated/**`, `products/*/frontend/generated/**`)
- Generation tooling changes (mutator, stub, generation script)

Runs the OpenAPI validation without triggering the full Django test suite.

| Change | OpenAPI check | Django tests |
|--------|---------------|--------------|
| Backend code | Yes | Yes |
| Generated files only | Yes | No |
| Generation tooling | Yes | No |
author: webjunkie

reviews.body: **No issues found** across 1 file

## Pull request overview This PR enhances the CI pipeline to validate OpenAPI type consistency when generated TypeScript files or generation tooling changes, addressing a gap where incorrect types could be merged without backend code modifications. **Key changes:** - Added `openapi_types` path filter to detect changes in generated types and generation scripts - Updated `check-migrations` job to trigger on OpenAPI type changes in addition to backend changes - Renamed job from "Validate Django and CH migrations" to "Validate migrations and OpenAPI types" --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

commits.message:
fix(ci): validate OpenAPI types when generated files change

Previously, the OpenAPI drift check only ran when backend code changed.
This left a gap where someone could submit incorrect generated types
without validation if no backend files were modified.

Added new `openapi_types` filter that triggers validation when
generated files or generation tooling change, without running the
full Django test suite.

title: fix(phai): generate insight schemas in isolated context
body: ## Problem The `schema_generator` was previously including the full conversation history and human messages in its context, making it less atomic than intended. This change aims to simplify the schema generator's input, ensuring it only receives the provided plan and a static prompt to generate a schema from that plan. ## Changes - **`prompts.py`**: Removed `NEW_PLAN_PROMPT` and `QUESTION_PROMPT`. The `PLAN_PROMPT` now explicitly includes the instruction "Generate a schema from this plan." - **`nodes.py`**: - Removed unused imports related to conversation history (`AIMessage`, `find_start_message`, `ArtifactRefMessage`). - Simplified `_construct_messages()` to only include the group mapping prompt, the plan with the static schema generation instruction, and the failover prompt (for retries). - Removed the `_get_insight_plan()` method and all conversation history reconstruction logic. - **`test/test_nodes.py`**: Updated existing tests to reflect the new atomic behavior and removed tests related to conversation history reconstruction. - Updated individual prompts of the schema generators. - Swapped the model from gpt-5.1 to 5.2 ## How did you test this code? Updated and ran existing unit tests in `test/test_nodes.py` to verify the new atomic behavior, including tests for group mapping, plan inclusion, and failover scenarios. ## Publish to changelog? no ---

title: fix(insights): Use immutable update for goal line changes
body:
## Summary
- Fixes goal line changes not being detected as modifications (UI shows "no changes to be saved")

## Root Cause
The `updateGoalLine` reducer was mutating the goal line object in place:
```js
const goalLines = [...state]
goalLines[goalLineIndex][key] = value  // ← mutation
```

This creates a shallow copy of the array but still references the same object, causing change detection to fail.

## Fix
Use `.map()` to create both a new array and a new object for the updated goal line:
```js
return state.map((goalLine, index) =>
    index === goalLineIndex ? { ...goalLine, [key]: value } : goalLine
)
```

This ensures proper immutable state updates that React/Kea can detect.

## Test Plan
1. Open an insight with an existing goal line
2. Edit the goal line's label or value
3. Verify the changes are recognized and can be saved

Closes #30324

title: chore(exported assets): Ensure `expires_after` is always set
body: ## Problem Whilst working on adding a new `failure_type` to our ExportedAssets to improve our observability, I noticed that by far the majority of `ExportedAssets` are being created without an `expires_after` being set: **US:** [link](https://metabase.prod-us.posthog.dev/question#) **EU:** [link](https://metabase.prod-eu.posthog.dev/question#) _(caveat that there is a survivorship bias here, as the majority of items with `expires_after` should have been removed already)_ This means that we are effectively not automatically deleting/cleaning those assets. Whilst looking through the code, I presume the majority of these cases are the result of the `bulk_create` functionality used in the `subscription` flow, which circumvents the `save()` hook (due to performance reasons) due to which the `expires_after` is never automatically filled. After investigating how the cleanup happens, it appears that, whilst there are a whole bunch of assets not being cleaned up, this is only affecting the database record. The cleanup of the "content" is being done over here: https://github.com/PostHog/posthog-cloud-infra/blob/333a174019b974871f1bd6bbf2fb48b3bde826e2/terraform/modules/posthog/s3/app_assets.tf#L88-L188 Therefore the "impact" of `expires_after` not being set here, is that: 1. We are cluttering our DB with records which have content that is no longer available. 2. Users might think they can still access the content, whilst in reality the underlying content has already been expired, leading to a sub optimal UX. This PR updates the `save()` logic so that it's always set, as we're also always expiring content. This aligns our code with the infra. ## Changes 1. Simplified `save()` to always set `expires_after` if missing (and add to `update_fields` for partial saves) 2. Fixed `bulk_create` gap in subscriptions by explicitly setting `expires_after` on asset construction 4. Added `backfill_exported_asset_expiry` management command for existing `NULL` records ## How did you test this code? ✅ Subscription runs still work and `expires_after` is populated: ✅ During single exports (dashboard / insights). the field is populated correctly as well: ✅ The backfill command works locally: _dry run:_ _live run with small batch size:_ _0 assets after the previous live run:_ ✅ Tests are passing 👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._ ## Publish to changelog? N/A

title: fix(orval): add alias stub to work around esbuild scss bundling
body:
**Problem**

Orval v8 uses esbuild internally to bundle mutator files for static analysis (parsing function signatures). When the mutator imports `lib/api`, esbuild follows the entire import chain and fails on ~400 scss files it can't handle. This causes orval to silently generate no output files.

**Fix**

Added a stub file (`api-stub-for-orval.ts`) that provides the same interface as `lib/api` but without the scss dependency chain. The orval config uses the `alias` option to redirect `lib/api` to this stub during code generation.

The stub is never executed at runtime - it's only used during orval's esbuild bundling step for signature parsing.

**Root cause**

Identified via git bisect: orval's "Convert to ESM" commit ([orval-labs/orval#2460](https://github.com/orval-labs/orval/pull/2460)) introduced esbuild for mutator validation.
author: webjunkie

reviews.body: **No issues found** across 25 files

## Pull request overview This PR fixes an issue where orval v8's esbuild bundling fails when processing mutator files that import `lib/api`, which has a dependency chain including ~400 SCSS files that esbuild cannot handle. **Key changes:** - Added a stub file (`api-stub-for-orval.ts`) that provides the same interface as `lib/api` without SCSS dependencies - Updated orval config to use an alias that redirects `lib/api` imports to the stub during code generation - Regenerated API type files (mostly reordering of type definitions) ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 1 out of 25 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `frontend/src/lib/api-stub-for-orval.ts` | New stub file providing the same API interface without SCSS dependencies | | `frontend/bin/generate-openapi-types.mjs` | Added alias configuration to redirect lib/api imports to the stub | | Generated API files (multiple) | Regenerated type definitions with reordered type declarations | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

commits.message:
fix(orval): add alias stub to work around esbuild scss bundling

Orval v8 uses esbuild to bundle mutator files for static analysis.
When the mutator imports lib/api, esbuild follows the entire import
chain and fails on scss files it can't handle.

This adds a stub file that provides the same interface as lib/api
but without the scss dependency chain. The alias config tells orval
to use the stub during code generation - it's never executed at runtime.

title: fix(ci): prevent cache branch divergence in master CI status
body:
**Problem**

Incidents were being duplicated instead of updated. Found another possible cause: GitHub cache prefix matching returns "most recently **created** cache", not the one with the latest state. When concurrent runs exist, older runs could create cache entries that get restored instead of the newer incident cache.

**Fix**

1. Delete ALL old caches when creating a new incident - wipes the slate clean
2. Don't save cache on "action: none" - prevents divergent branches from "no-op" runs
author: webjunkie

reviews.body: **No issues found** across 2 files

## Pull request overview This pull request fixes a cache divergence issue in the master CI status tracking workflow where concurrent runs could create multiple incident cache entries, causing stale data to be restored due to GitHub's "most recently created cache" prefix matching behavior. **Key Changes:** - Added cache cleanup step that deletes all old incident caches when creating a new incident - Modified cache save logic to only save when state changes meaningfully (on create/update/resolve), preventing "no-op" runs from creating divergent cache branches ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | .github/workflows/ci-master-status.yml | Added "Delete old caches" step that removes all previous incident caches when creating a new incident, ensuring only one cache exists | | .github/scripts/master-ci-status.js | Added delete_old_caches output for new incidents; modified save_cache logic to only save on meaningful state changes (not on action='none' unless resolved) | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

commits.message:
fix(ci): prevent cache branch divergence in master CI status

Root cause: With per-run cache keys, concurrent workflow runs could
create parallel cache "branches". GitHub's restore-keys prefix matching
returns "most recently created cache", not "cache with latest state".
This caused runs to keep restoring old state and creating new incidents
instead of updating existing ones.

Fix:
1. Delete ALL old caches when creating a new incident, ensuring the new
   incident cache becomes the single source of truth
2. Don't save cache on "action: none" (successes that don't change state)
   to prevent creating divergent branches in the first place

The combination ensures only meaningful state changes create caches,
and new incidents wipe old caches to prevent divergence.

title: feat: send issue spiking event
body: # Overview Added basic spikes detection mechanism. If it can, it computes baseline based on the historical data related to this particular issue. If there is not enough data, it computes baseline based on overall statistics based on team. For now we will enable it only for team 2. PR here -> https://github.com/PostHog/charts/pull/7749 ## New data in redis Added some data to the redis. ### A Up until now we were recording exceptions per issue per time bucket in this format ### B Now additionally we are recording number of exceptions per team id per time bucket ### C And we also store a set of issue ids per team id per time bucket ### Why? We need these new stats to compute baseline for new issues which do not have any history. Based on B and C we are able to tell how many exceptions per issue per time bucket we had on average and then we can do average of those averages to get average number of exceptions per issue per 5m # Per issue baseline This applies to issues which have some history. By some history I mean there is at least 1 bucket different than current bucket which we can use to compute a baseline for that particular issue. If issue has been happening for more than 5 minutes, we can use that method for this issue (because we have 5 minute buckets so it means now we have at least 2 buckets) We compute the baseline as an average of last 12 (excluding current) non empty buckets. If we get 10x the baseline, we fire an event # Team-average baseline If this is a new issue, we have no data to compare against so we are using what I described in B and C to construct team average and compare against it. Same -> if we get 10x the baseline, we fire an event # How I tested it 1. Looked at the local redis to validate whether all keys are there as expected 2. Checked local kafka ui (kafka-ui.posthog.orb.local if you're using orbstack) to make sure that spike events are being sent but also to make sure that the cooldown mechanism works 3. Checked cymbal logs (I removed these logs later to not spam) 4. Tested new env config by adding some team ids to `bin/start-rust-service` and making sure that we don't emit events for teams which are not whitelisted.

This inconsistency means tests cannot simulate error scenarios. Consider adding a `batch_sadd_expire_ret` field and setter following the established pattern.</violation>

<sub>Reply with feedback, questions, or to request a fix. Tag `@cubic-dev-ai` to re-run a review.</sub>

## Pull request overview

This PR implements a spike detection mechanism for error tracking issues. When an issue experiences a 10x increase compared to its baseline (computed from historical data or team averages), a `$error_tracking_issue_spiking` event is emitted. The feature includes a cooldown mechanism to prevent alert spam and is initially enabled only for whitelisted teams.

**Key changes:**
- Implements spike detection using 5-minute time buckets with baselines computed from either per-issue historical data (last 12 non-empty buckets) or team-wide averages when insufficient history exists
- Adds Redis tracking for issue counts, team exception counts, and unique issue sets per time bucket to support baseline calculations
- Introduces event emission with cooldown (10 minutes) to prevent duplicate alerts for the same issue

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 9 out of 9 changed files in this pull request and generated 7 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| rust/cymbal/src/pipeline/exception/spike_detection.rs | New module implementing spike detection algorithm, baseline computation, event emission, and comprehensive test suite |
| rust/cymbal/src/pipeline/exception/mod.rs | Integrates spike detection into exception handling pipeline, replacing simple bucket tracking |
| rust/cymbal/src/lib.rs | Removes old issue_buckets module replaced by spike_detection |
| rust/cymbal/src/issue_buckets.rs | Deleted file - functionality moved and expanded in spike_detection module |
| rust/cymbal/src/config.rs | Adds configuration for team whitelist to control spike alert rollout |
| rust/common/redis/src/client.rs | Implements scard_multiple and batch_sadd_expire for efficient Redis set operations |
| rust/common/redis/src/read_write.rs | Adds read-write client support for new Redis operations with fallback logic |
| rust/common/redis/src/mock.rs | Adds mock implementations for new Redis

```text
P1: Cooldown lock is acquired before Kafka send but not released on failure. If Kafka fails, the spike alert won't be sent AND won't be retried for 10 minutes because the lock persists. Consider either releasing the lock on Kafka failure, or reverting to the old pattern of setting cooldowns only after successful sends.</violation>
```

title: refactor(cohorts): use query for duplicating existing cohorts
body:
## Problem

We are calling `insert_cohort_from_insight_filter` when duplicating a cohort. This in turn calls `insert_cohort_actors_into_ch`, which contains [legacy insight calculations code](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/blob/master/posthog/api/cohort.py#L1467-L1502). This method is not used anywhere besides the cohort duplication any more. 

## Changes

We want to get rid of the legacy insight calculations code. I could just have removed the now unused code path related to that, but decided to go a step further and replace the cohort calculation with the modern query-based variant.

This is PR 1/2 where I replace the frontend side call with the query-based approach. PR 2/2 removes the legacy code: https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/44481

## How did you test this code?

Duplicated a static and a dynamic cohort locally. Adapted tests in the follow up PR.

title: fix: Bump max record batch size for Redshift (INSERT)
body:
## Problem

`max_record_batch_size_bytes` is set low in Redshift (INSERT) as there is a relatively tight max query size that we must follow.

However, the value of `max_record_batch_size_bytes` is inconsequential for this: The transformer already splits up record batches into individual records, processing one at a time. Whether the record batch is 2MB or 10MB won't make it go over the query size limit, as the transformer will cut off the stream at the individual record level.

I *think* that since we slice record batches to really small sizes in a synchronous fashion in the code we may not be allowing requests from S3 to resume quickly enough, so I want to try bumping the `max_record_batch_size_bytes` to avoid frequent slicing.

<!-- Who are we building for, what are their needs, why is this important? -->

<!-- Closes #ISSUE_ID -->

## Changes

Bump `max_record_batch_size_bytes` to 10MB for Redshift (INSERT). May bump this more later.

title: refactor(exporter): Clean-up unused / redundant PromQL counters"
body: ## Problem Whilst working on improving our error tracking / visibility of our export / subscription flow, I noticed that we have a bunch of PromQL counters which either: - Are redundant (and superseded by other metrics) - Unused in dashboards / queries - Use different values for their fields, making it impossible (or at least very dev-unfriendly) to calculate any meaningful results across metrics as the fields do not map nicely. This would be hard to spot as there would be overlap for the majority of cases, however for `xlsx` we do not increment `EXPORT_QUEUED_COUNTER/EXPORT_FAILED_COUNTER` correctly whilst we do set it correctly in `EXPORT_SUCCEEDED_COUNTER`. Additionally this also makes it so that we have a single source of truth instead of having strings defined in different places (which could potentially mismatch). As per the boyscout principle, I've taken the liberty to clean these up. (Depends on: https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/44233 and https://github.com/PostHog/posthog-cloud-infra/pull/6210) (Also depends on a fix to export PromQL metrics from Temporal: https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/44311) ## Changes - Use `exported_asset.export_format` for the `type` field in the different PromQL metrics to ensure consistency - Removed the `EXPORT_ASSET_UNKNOWN_COUNTER` which was only used in the `csv` flow, but has no dashboard usage. - Removed `EXPORT_QUEUED_COUNTER` in favour of `EXPORT_SUCCEEDED_COUNTER`, as both were effectively incremented one after another, and therefore one is sufficient. - Renamed the `celery_task` field to `task` in the captured exception flow, as this task can be triggered through both celery (one off) and temporarl (subscription). - Centralised counter increments to keep the logic in the top level and remove the need for the application code to be aware of our observability flow. ## How did you test this code? **`EXPORT_ASSET_UNKNOWN_COUNTER` is unused** **`EXPORT_QUEUED_COUNTER` is unused** The usage in the first 2 screenshots will be removed once: https://github.com/PostHog/posthog-cloud-infra/pull/6210 has been merged, as the `succeeded` metric effectively `supersedes` the queued one. **Counters still get incremented correctly after a single export run** _before:_ _after:_ (ran it a few times with different formats) **Counters still get incremented correctly after a subscription run** _before:_ _after:_ 👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._ ## Publish to changelog?

title: fix(dags): dagster job for reconciling missed person prop updates
body:
## Problem

A bug was introduced to the ingestion pipeline which resulted in the dropping of person property updates

## Changes

A dagster job that is outlined as follows:

Parent task:

* Fetch all teams from CH events effected by the incident window
* Farm out one child job (with capped pool) for every team effected

Child task:

* Query CH for all all persons effected for 1 team
* Run single person update logic in loop

title: fix(exports): include dashboard tile filter overrides in query
body: ## Problem When exporting a dashboard as an image, tile-level filter overrides are not applied to the final generated image even though the title says "Tile filters applied". The insight data in the export uses dashboard-level filters but ignores tile-specific filter overrides. Reported by this ticket: https://posthoghelp.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/45548 ## Changes Include tile filter overrides when calculating the query. I used`calculate_for_query_based_insight` because it already contains the title filter override logic, so I use it instead of adding the logic before`process_query_dict`. ## How did you test this code? Dashboard UI: Export before change: - Notice how the title contains "Tile filters applied" but the start date of the insight is wrong compared to the UI. Export after change: - Notice how the start date is now correct Note: this works when exporting the entire dashboard and also when exporting the individual insight from the dashboard view. However, the tile filter override is not in the image when exporting from the insight UI when the tile override is the query params (i.e. when you click into an insight with a tile filter override and get a url with `tile_filters_override` like the one below ). I'll make a follow-up pr to address that. ` http://localhost:8010/project/4/insights/yaS3WZFB?dashboard=9&variables_override=%7B%7D&filters_override=%7B%7D&tile_filters_override=%7B%22date_from%22%3A%22-7d%22%2C%22explicitDate%22%3Afalse%7D ` 👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._ ## Publish to changelog?

title: feat: support creating cohorts from queries with distinct_id
body:
## Problem

When creating a static cohort from a HogQL query that returns a `distinct_id` column instead of `person_id`, `actor_id`, or `id`, the cohort creation would fail with an error: "ValueError: Could not find a person_id, actor_id, or id column in the query"

This was particularly problematic for queries like `SELECT * FROM <view>` where the view contains `distinct_id` but not `person_id`.

## Changes

- Modified `print_cohort_hogql_query` to detect `distinct_id` columns and automatically wrap queries with a lookup to `person_distinct_id2` table
- Updated modal descriptions to inform users that `distinct_id` is now supported and will be automatically resolved to person_id
- Added debug logging to show which ID column is being used

## How did you test this code?

1. Added test in `test_cohort.py` for queries with `distinct_id` column
2. Verified existing functionality (queries with `person_id`, `actor_id`, `id`) still works
3. Tested fallback logic for `SELECT *` and `FROM events/persons` queries

## Publish to changelog?

No.
author: gustavohstrassburger

fix: update static cohort modal to mention distinct_id support
fix: preserve HogQL settings when wrapping distinct_id queries

fix: detect and reject UNION queries with mixed ID types in cohort calculations

When creating static cohorts from insight queries, UNION queries that mix
person_id/actor_id with distinct_id could produce incorrect results. The
wrapper query expects all rows to have distinct_id, but some would output
actor_id, causing the person_distinct_id2 lookup to fail silently.

Changes:
- Add validation to detect and reject UNION queries with mixed ID types
- Track both uses_actor_id and uses_distinct_id flags separately
- Raise clear error message when mixed ID types are detected
- Remove incorrect assumption that SELECT * means distinct_id is available
- Replace fragile SETTINGS clause string parsing with dict formatting
- Add comprehensive test coverage for edge cases

title: feat: limit query cache size per team
body:
## Problem Query cache warming is unbounded, and a single overly active team can end up using a significant amount of our Redis cache memory. See https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C0A5PTJHPST/p1767118855343279?thread_ts=1767116577.303139&cid=C0A5PTJHPST ## Changes - Adds per-team cache size limits (default 500MB) with LRU eviction to prevent any single team from consuming excessive memory New module: posthog/caching/cache_size_tracker.py - TeamCacheSizeTracker class tracks cache usage per team using 3 Redis keys: - Sorted set for LRU ordering (member=cache_key, score=timestamp) - Hash for O(1) size lookups (field=cache_key, value=size) - String counter for O(1) total size reads - When cache writes would exceed the limit, evicts oldest entries until under limit - Lazy cleanup handles TTL-expired keys during eviction - TTL on tracking keys (~8 days) ensures they auto-expire for inactive teams - get_team_cache_limit() supports per-team overrides via Team.extra_settings["cache_size_limit_bytes"] Note: the actual memory usage is lower because the query results are compressed before storing in Redis ## How did you test this code? - Verified locally that tracking keys are created on query cache write and evicted when a new cache write is above the limit Here is the script I used to test against my local instance. Make sure to change API_TOKEN and TEAM_ID.
local testing script ``` #!/usr/bin/env python """ E2E test: Verify cache tracking keys are created and evicted. Usage: PYTHONPATH=. python test_cache_size_tracker_e2e.py """ import os os.environ.setdefault("DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE", "posthog.settings") import django django.setup() import requests from django.core.cache import cache from posthog.caching.cache_size_tracker import TeamCacheSizeTracker, get_team_cache_limit from posthog.models import Insight, Team BASE_URL = "http://localhost:8000" API_TOKEN = "" TEAM_ID = 4 def load_insight(insight_id: int) -> int: url = f"{BASE_URL}/api/environments/{TEAM_ID}/insights/{insight_id}/" headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {API_TOKEN}"} return requests.get(url, headers=headers, params={"refresh": "force_blocking"}, timeout=60).status_code def get_tracked_keys(tracker: TeamCacheSizeTracker) -> set[str]: members = tracker.redis_client.zrange(tracker.entries_key, 0, -1) return {m.decode() if isinstance(m, bytes) else m for m in members} def main(): team = Team.objects.get(pk=TEAM_ID) insights = list(Insight.objects.filter(team=team, deleted=False)[:10]) assert len(insights) >= 3, f"Need at least 3 insights, found {len(insights)}" tracker = TeamCacheSizeTracker(team.pk) original_settings = team.extra_settings.copy() if team.extra_settings else {} try: tracker.purge() # Load insights until we have at least 2 unique cache keys tracked_keys: set[str] = set() for insight in insights: load_insight(insight.pk) tracked_keys = get_tracked_keys(tracker) print(f" Loaded insight {insight.pk}, tracked keys: {len(tracked_keys)}") if len(tracked_keys) >= 2: break assert len(tracked_keys) >= 2, f"Need at least 2 unique cache keys, got {len(tracked_keys)}" print(f"✓ {len(tracked_keys)} tracking keys created") # Remember the oldest key (first in sorted set) oldest_key = tracker.redis_client.zrange(tracker.entries_key, 0, 0)[0] if isinstance(oldest_key, bytes): oldest_key = oldest_key.decode() print(f" Oldest key: {oldest_key[:40]}...") # Set limit to force eviction on next write current_size = tracker.get_total_size() team.extra_settings = {**original_settings, "cache_size_limit_bytes": current_size + 100} team.save() get_team_cache_limit._cache.clear() print(f" Set limit to {current_size + 100} (current: {current_size})") # Load another insight - should evict oldest for insight in insights: keys_before = get_tracked_keys(tracker) if len(keys_before) >= 2: load_insight(insight.pk) keys_after = get_tracked_keys(tracker) if oldest_key not in keys_after: break assert oldest_key not in get_tracked_keys(tracker), "Oldest key should have been evicted from tracking" assert cache.get(oldest_key) is None, "Oldest key should be removed from Django cache" print(f"✓ Oldest key evicted from tracking and cache") print(f"\nPASSED") finally: team.extra_settings = original_settings team.save() get_team_cache_limit._cache.clear() if __name__ == "__main__": main() ```
👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._ ## Publish to changelog?

Consider using `logger.debug` for routine writes, or only logging at INFO level when eviction occurs (i.e., when `evicted_count > 0`). The previous implementation wisely only logged when evictions happened.</violation>
```text
</file>
```

title: feat(flags): fix tagging UX
body:
## Problem

The `FeatureFlagEvaluationTags` component was tightly coupled to `SceneTags`-style "save immediately" behavior, making it awkward to use in forms where changes should be staged until the user saves the whole flag.
Also, the UX for adding evaluation tags was clunky - users had to click a separate button to reveal the evaluation tag options, then check boxes, then save. Two extra clicks for a simple operation.

## Changes

- Added `onChange` prop for form usage where parent manages state (changes are staged)
- Kept `onSave` prop for `SceneTags`-style immediate saves
- Removed the toggle button for evaluation options - checkboxes are now always visible when tags exist
- Fixed state not updating after save by calling `setFeatureFlag` in `saveFeatureFlagSuccess`

### How did you test this code?

- Tested form mode (onChange): changes stage correctly, saved with flag
Tested direct save mode (onSave): saves immediately
- Verified evaluation tag checkboxes appear inline when adding tags

title: fix: use CLICKHOUSE_MAX_MEMORY setting when executing queries
body:
Follow up to #44163 to use `get_client` so that `CLICKHOUSE_MAX_MEMORY` is used.

After stopping using the `get_client`, some queries are failing due to memory limit.

```
Code: 241. DB::Exception: Query memory limit exceeded: would use 42.00 GiB (attempt to allocate chunk of 4.50 MiB bytes), maximum: 42.00 GiB: While executing AggregatingTransform. (MEMORY_LIMIT_EXCEEDED) (version 25.8.11.66 (official build))
```
author: gustavohstrassburger

reviews.body: ## Pull request overview This PR addresses memory limit issues by replacing `sync_execute` with `get_client` to properly apply the `CLICKHOUSE_MAX_MEMORY` setting when executing ClickHouse queries in Temporal workflows. The change prevents queries from exceeding memory limits (e.g., 42 GiB) that were occurring after a previous refactoring. **Key Changes:** - Migrated from synchronous `sync_execute` to asynchronous `get_client` for ClickHouse query execution - Added `FORMAT JSONEachRow` to queries and implemented JSON parsing of results to match the new client's response format - Removed unnecessary `asyncio` import from one file where it's no longer needed after the migration ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `posthog/temporal/messaging/realtime_cohort_calculation_workflow.py` | Replaced `sync_execute` with async `get_client` for cohort calculation queries, added JSON parsing with graceful error handling that continues processing on malformed lines | | `posthog/temporal/messaging/backfill_precalculated_person_properties_coordinator_workflow.py` | Replaced `sync_execute` with async `get_client` for person count queries, added JSON parsing with strict error handling that raises on parse failures, removed unused `asyncio` import | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
**No issues found** across 2 files

## Pull request overview Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

Changes as-is looks good to me.

However, I noticed this PR uses `read_query()` instead of `stream_query_as_jsonl()`, which makes sense given the `JSONDecodeError` from PR #44338:
 
> Unterminated string starting at: line 1 column 200539 (char 200538)

I looked into the root cause - there's a bug in `stream_query_as_jsonl` (lines 691-714 in `posthog/temporal/common/clickhouse.py`). The current implementation assumes every HTTP chunk contains at least one complete JSON line:

```python
async for chunk in response.content.iter_any():
    lines = chunk.split(line_separator)
    yield json.loads(buffer + lines[0])  # BUG: assumes this is complete!
```

When a chunk splits mid-JSON (e.g., `{"status": "ent | ered"}`), it fails.

The fix is simple - buffer until there's a complete line:

```python
buffer = b""
async with self.apost_query(...) as response:
    async for chunk in response.content.iter_any():
        buffer += chunk
        while line_separator in buffer:
            line, buffer = buffer.split(line_separator, 1)
            if line:
                yield json.loads(line)
    if buffer.strip():
        yield json.loads(buffer)
```

This PR is fine as-is with `read_query()`, but if you're interested in fixing the underlying streaming bug, it would allow better memory efficiency for large cohorts (streaming processes rows incrementally vs buffering the entire response).

commits.message: perf: use CLICKHOUSE_MAX_MEMORY setting when executing queries
refactor: added protection for invalid json

title: feat(analytics-platform): Fix poor performance for is_not_set by removing jsonhas
body:
## Problem
When using `is_not_set` we compare against NULL, but we also add a `JSONHas` operation to the query. This is terrible for performance with materialized columns, as it means we need to touch the massive `properties` object, which somewhat defeats the point of using a materialized column.

## Changes
I was a bit cautious about removing the `JSONHas`, as I was worried that it would cause inconsistent behaviour when the column was/wasn't materialized. It turns out that we have inconsistent behaviour anyway, so let's fix performance while not changing the existing behaviour.

## How did you test this code?

See `test_person_property_is_not_set_behavior`

## Pull request overview

This PR improves query performance for the `is_not_set` operator by removing the `JSONHas` operation that was previously combined with NULL comparison. When using materialized columns, the `JSONHas` operation forces the query to access the large properties JSON object, defeating the performance benefits of materialization. The change simplifies `is_not_set` to only check for NULL values, which maintains existing behavior while significantly improving performance with materialized columns.

title: perf(flags): Optimize experience continuity lookups for flags that don't need them
body:
Skip hash key override database lookups for flags with experience continuity enabled when they don't actually need them.

## Problem

Feature flags that have experience continuity enabled (aka "Persist flag across authentication steps" is checked) require additional queries to look up hash key overrides in the database. However, there are situations where flags don't actually need this work even though experience continuity is enabled.

Flags at 100% rollout with no multivariate variants return the same value for everyone, so the hash bucket doesn't affect the result


## Changes

- Add `OPTIMIZE_EXPERIENCE_CONTINUITY_LOOKUPS` config (default: false)
- Add helper methods: `has_experience_continuity()`, `has_hash_dependent_variants()`, `has_partial_rollout()`, `needs_hash_key_override()`
- Add metric with status label for observability: `flags_experience_continuity_optimized_total{status="skipped|eligible"}`
- Add comprehensive test coverage with lookup verification

## Context

The experience continuity lookup happens once per request if __any__ flag with `ensure_experience_continuity=true` needs it. The optimization skips this lookup only when __all__ continuity-enabled flags are at 100% rollout with no multivariate variants.

Query to identify teams that can benefit:

```sql
WITH continuity_flags AS (
    SELECT
        team_id,
        CASE
            WHEN jsonb_array_length(COALESCE(filters->'multivariate'->'variants', '[]'::jsonb)) > 0
            THEN true ELSE false
        END as has_variants,
        CASE
            WHEN EXISTS (
                SELECT 1 FROM jsonb_array_elements(COALESCE(filters->'groups', '[]'::jsonb)) as g
                WHERE (g->>'rollout_percentage')::numeric < 100
            )
            THEN true ELSE false
        END as has_partial_rollout
    FROM posthog_featureflag
    WHERE ensure_experience_continuity = true
        AND active = true
        AND deleted = false
),
team_summary AS (
    SELECT
        team_id,
        bool_and(NOT (has_variants OR has_partial_rollout)) as can_skip_lookup
    FROM continuity_flags
    GROUP BY team_id
)
SELECT
    COUNT(*) as teams_with_continuity,
    SUM(CASE WHEN can_skip_lookup THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as teams_that_can_skip,
    ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN can_skip_lookup THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 1) as pct_optimizable
FROM team_summary;
```

Results:

```text
| Region | Teams with continuity | Teams that can skip | % Optimizable |
|--------|-----------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| US     | 3,042                 | 1,415               | 46.5%         |
| EU     | 1,400                 | 632                 | 45.1%         |
```

Conclusion

~46% of teams using experience continuity can skip the database lookup entirely. This represents ~2,047 teams that will avoid a DB query on every `/flags` request when the optimization is enabled.

The common pattern: teams enabled continuity for A/B tests, rolled out to 100%, but left the setting enabled. This optimization handles that automatically.

## How did you test this code?

Unit Tests
Manual Testing

👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._

## Publish to changelog?

No

commits.message:
Optimize experience continuity lookups for flags that don't need them

Skip hash key override database lookups for experience continuity flags
that don't actually need them (100% rollout with no multivariate variants,
or multivariate flags where one variant is at 100%).

Key changes:
- Add config flag OPTIMIZE_EXPERIENCE_CONTINUITY_LOOKUPS (default: false)
- Add helper methods: has_experience_continuity(), needs_hash_key_override()
- Build dependency graph early and reuse for both optimization check and evaluation
- Remove redundant feature_flags parameter from evaluate_flags_with_overrides
- Add hash_key_override_skipped field to canonical log
- Add metric to track optimization (status=skipped|eligible)
- Comprehensive test coverage for the optimization

Improve comments based on code review

Co-authored-by: Copilot <175728472+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

Fix experience continuity count to respect flag_keys filter

The flags_experience_continuity metric was incorrectly counted before
flag_keys filtering, causing mismatches like flags_evaluated=2 but
flags_experience_continuity=3 when requesting specific flags.

Move the count into evaluate_all_feature_flags where we have the
filtered dependency graph, ensuring the count reflects the flags
that will actually be evaluated.

Remove duplicate dependency graph error logging

Errors were being logged twice: once inside build_dependency_graph()
and again in evaluate_all_feature_flags(). Keep only the logging in
build_dependency_graph() and remove the redundant call.

title: feat(llma): add error normalization at ingestion time
body:
## Context

I have been digging in a bit to the current query on the fly that powers the errors tab and it just does not scale well for bigger users. One option would be to try use `SAMPLE` in some way but just doing the regexes on the fly is bad and not really future proof or easy to grow and evolve the logic over time. It was fine to make the POC but in order to move out of alpha i think it makes much more sense to just do a little bit of light wrangling on ingestion to generate a `$ai_error_normalized` and then the errors sql if very simple and already using the materialized `$ai_is_error` col so ends up just a simplie enough agg. 

So idea here is to do that, and refactor the code structure a little since we would now have both costs and errors relatete wrangling at ingestion time.

Plan is to have this running for ~14 days and then it would be easy to move out of alpha as about 14 days ingestion should be enough for a decent errors tab experience for most users.

## Problem

The LLM analytics errors tab runs expensive SQL with 15 sequential regex operations at query time. This is slow for large users.

## Changes

Move error normalization from query time to ingestion time in the plugin server:

- Restructure `ai-costs/` to `ai/` with `costs/` and `errors/` submodules
- Add `$ai_error_normalized` property to AI events with `$ai_is_error=true`
- Normalize request IDs, UUIDs, timestamps, tool call IDs, user IDs, token counts, and numbers
- Truncate long error messages to 1000 chars before normalizing (avoids storage bloat)
- Respect user-provided `$ai_error_normalized` if already set (skip our normalization) - a new feature/option some users might actually want potentially.
- JSON.stringify object/array errors instead of producing "[object Object]"
- Simplify `errors.sql` from 239 lines (15 CTEs) to 37 lines using the pre-computed property
- New events only, no backfill required

## How did you test this code?

- Unit tests for all normalization patterns (64 tests passing)
- Manual testing: ingested AI events with errors and verified `$ai_error_normalized` property in ClickHouse
- Verified errors tab works correctly in local PostHog

## Publish to changelog?

no

commits.message:
feat(llma): add error normalization at ingestion time

Move AI event processing from ai-costs/ to ai/ with better structure:
- ai/costs/ - cost calculation logic
- ai/errors/ - error normalization logic
- ai/process-ai-event.ts - main orchestrator

Add $ai_error_normalized property to AI events with errors, normalizing:
- Request IDs (req_xxx) and UUIDs → <ID>
- ISO timestamps → <TIMESTAMP>
- GCP paths → projects/<PATH>
- Tool call IDs → <TOOL_CALL_ID>
- Function call IDs → call_<CALL_ID>
- User IDs → user_<USER_ID>
- Token counts → <TOKEN_COUNT>
- Status codes and numbers → <N>

This moves expensive regex normalization from query time (ClickHouse SQL)
to ingestion time, improving errors tab performance for large users.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(llma): handle object errors and tokenCount whitespace

- JSON.stringify object/array $ai_error instead of String() which produces "[object Object]"
- Allow optional whitespace in tokenCount pattern ("tokenCount": 123)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(llma): respect user-provided $ai_error_normalized

Skip normalization if the user already provides their own
$ai_error_normalized value in the event properties.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

feat(llma): simplify errors.sql to use pre-computed $ai_error_normalized

Replaces the 15-step CTE normalization pipeline with direct property
extraction. The normalization is now done at ingestion time in the
plugin server, significantly reducing query complexity and cost.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

feat(llma): truncate long error messages before normalization

Limits error messages to 1000 chars before normalizing to avoid
storage bloat from very long stack traces or JSON payloads.
Most useful error info is at the beginning anyway.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

feat(llma): simplify error filtering to use $ai_error_normalized

Replace complex token-splitting with IContains filters on $ai_error
with a single exact match on $ai_error_normalized. Keep $ai_is_error
filter to leverage the index.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(llma): escape quotes in error filter to match HogQL extraction

HogQL's property access uses JSONExtractRaw with quote stripping, which
preserves internal backslash escapes. The errors.sql query uses
JSONExtractString which returns clean values. This mismatch caused
clicking on an error row to fail to find matching traces.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(llma): also escape backslashes in error filter

Error messages commonly contain backslash characters (e.g., file paths,
escape sequences). The previous fix only escaped quotes, which would
cause filter mismatches for errors with backslashes.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

refactor(llma): simplify error tests to test query aggregation

The error normalization logic has moved to Node.js ingestion and is
tested there (normalize-error.test.ts). These Python tests now focus
on verifying the errors.sql query correctly:
- Groups events by $ai_error_normalized
- Counts event types (generations, spans, embeddings)
- Counts unique traces, sessions, and users

Use generic type parameter to preserve input type and avoid cast at call site.
Add try-catch to ensure error normalization failures don't affect cost processing.

title: fix(batch-exports): Improve batch export query handling
body:
## Problem

When executing batch export queries we can encounter various ClickHouse errors that need proper handling. Previously:
- Query timeout handling was mixed with the main execution logic
- ClickHouse could return errors as JSON with 200 status codes (e.g., `{"exception": "..."}`) which weren't properly detected
- When checking the query status we had no way to differentiate between errors in the original query and errors in fetching the original query.

## Changes

- Added new `ClickHouseCheckQueryStatusError` exception to differentiate errors that occur while checking query status from errors in the query itself
- Added `read_query_as_jsonl` helper method to parse JSONEachRow responses
- Improved `acheck_query_in_query_log` to:
  - Detect JSON error responses from ClickHouse (when a single row with only "exception" key is returned)
  - Properly wrap errors when checking query status
- Improved `acheck_query_in_process_list` to wrap errors when checking
- Refactored batch export query execution into separate `_execute_query` and `_wait_for_query_completion` functions for better separation of concerns
- Made `ClickHouseCheckQueryStatusError` retryable in the wait loop, so transient errors during status checks don't fail the whole export

## How did you test this code?

- Added unit test `test_acheck_query_in_query_log_error` that verifies the new error handling when ClickHouse returns a JSON error response with 200 status
- Existing tests continue to pass

## Publish to changelog?

No

Differentiate between ClickHouse errors

Currently we don't know if the underlying query failed or the query to
fetch the query status failed, so here we differentiate between the two.

Handle JSON errors from ClickHouse.

Apply suggestions from code review

title: feat(logs): add event tracking for activation metric discovery
body:
## Problem

We need to discover the activation metric for Logs. Per the [PostHog activation metrics guide](https://posthog.com/product-engineers/activation-metrics), this requires:

1. Tracking candidate events that might "hook" users
2. Waiting 3-6 months for data
3. Analyzing which event combinations correlate with 3-month retention
4. Implementing `has_activated_logs()` with the winning criteria

Currently, Logs has no event tracking - we can't discover the activation metric without data.

## Changes

**Event tracking** (`posthog.capture`) for candidate activation signals:

```
// logsLogic.tsx - query/filter layer
posthog.capture('logs query executed', { has_search_term, has_filters, severity_count, service_count })
posthog.capture('logs results returned', { count })
posthog.capture('logs no results returned')
posthog.capture('logs filter changed', { filter_type: 'search' | 'attributes' | 'severity' | 'service' | 'date_range', ... })
posthog.capture('logs live tail started')
posthog.capture('logs load more requested')

// logsViewerLogic.ts - viewer layer
posthog.capture('logs log expanded')
posthog.capture('logs log pinned')
posthog.capture('logs link copied')
posthog.capture('logs bulk copy', { count })
posthog.capture('logs exported', { format, count })
posthog.capture('logs column added', { attribute_key })

// logDetailsModalLogic.ts - details modal
posthog.capture('logs details opened')
posthog.capture('logs details tab changed', { tab })

// logExploreAILogic.ts
posthog.capture('logs ai explanation requested')
```

Filter events use `hasRunQuery` guard to avoid tracking on initial page load from URL sync. Clearing filters is also tracked (e.g., `service_count: 0`).

**Product intent contexts** - we now have 2:

- `LOGS_DOCS_VIEWED` (existing) - onboarding interest
- `LOGS_SET_FILTERS` (new) - fired on all filter-related actions so `contexts.get("logs_set_filters")` gives total filter interaction count for activation checking

**Kea refactor** - extracted URL sync into dedicated actions (`syncUrlAndRunQuery`, `syncUrlWithPageSize`, `syncUrlWithHighlight`) to avoid a conflict where having both a `listener` and `tabAwareActionToUrl` handler for the same action (e.g., `setDateRange`) prevented the URL from updating. Filter actions now trigger listeners which call the sync actions.

## How did you test this code?

- Verified events fire correctly by checking browser console/network
- Confirmed URL sync works after refactor
- Ran kea typegen to confirm no type errors
- Schema regenerated successfully

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Changes

We had a 25K limit (see #24754) due to inefficient calculations when converting limits into max limited usage count - some customers have ran into this limitation and asked for higher limits.

Since then we made some other significant performance improvements in billing API so these inefficiencies are now less magnified, so we can increase it. We will be switching over to a new calculation method soon anyway so we'll be able to remove these limits altogether.

For more context see: https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C08ERRQT41E/p1767796026394229

title: fix(ci): prevent cache race condition in master CI status
body:
**Fixes cache race condition causing duplicate Slack incidents**

When multiple CI workflows complete concurrently, the delete-before-save cache pattern caused races where one run would delete the cache while another was trying to restore it, leading to:
- "Cache not found" errors
- New incidents created instead of updating existing ones
- Orphaned Slack threads

**Changes:**

1. Use unique cache keys per run with `restore-keys` prefix matching - each run writes its own cache and reads the most recent
2. Use commit timestamp for incident start - better aligns pendingWorkflows check with actual failure timing  
3. Mark incidents as `resolved: true` in state file instead of deleting cache - avoids race on cleanup, lets cache expire naturally
author: webjunkie

reviews.body: **No issues found** across 2 files

## Pull request overview This PR addresses cache race conditions in the master CI status workflow that were causing duplicate Slack incident notifications when multiple workflows completed concurrently. The fix changes from a delete-before-save cache pattern to using unique cache keys with prefix matching, and marks resolved incidents with a flag instead of deleting cache entries. **Key Changes:** - Replaced fixed cache key with unique per-run keys using `github.run_id` and prefix matching for restoration - Changed incident resolution to mark state as `resolved: true` instead of deleting cache entries - Updated incident start time to use commit timestamp instead of current time for more accurate tracking ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `.github/workflows/ci-master-status.yml` | Implements unique cache keys per run with prefix matching and removes all cache deletion logic | | `.github/scripts/master-ci-status.js` | Uses commit timestamp for incident start, marks resolved incidents with a flag, and always saves cache for resolved incidents | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

commits.message:
fix(ci): prevent cache race condition in master status workflow

The delete-before-save pattern caused race conditions where concurrent
runs would find no cache and create duplicate incidents. Evidence from
logs showed "Cache not found" at 14:22:47 when another run had just
deleted the cache.

Changes:
- Use unique cache keys (`master-ci-incident-<run_id>`) instead of
  static key to avoid delete-before-save gap
- Use `restore-keys` prefix matching to get most recent cache
- Use commit timestamp for incident start time instead of wall clock,
  so pendingWorkflows check compares against when failure was authored
- Clean up all matching caches only on full resolution

fix(ci): mark incidents resolved in state instead of deleting cache

Instead of deleting caches on resolution (which caused race conditions),
mark incidents as `resolved: true` in the state file. When loading state,
treat resolved incidents as no active incident. Let cache expiry handle
cleanup naturally.

Changes:
- Set `state.resolved = true` when fully resolved
- Check for resolved flag when loading state
- Always save cache (even on resolution)
- Remove file deletion and `gh cache list` cleanup steps

title: feat(customers): Configure customer profile content
body: ## Problem This PR adds the ability to customize person profiles by implementing a configuration system that allows users to select which tiles to display in the profile view. This enhances the flexibility of the customer profile interface, giving users more control over what information they see. This is the first step towards a fully customizable customer profile. For now, we'll only allow changes in the "base" tiles, but the goal is to support arbitrary content (insights, etc) to be persisted as well. Closes https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/issues/44279 ## Changes - Created a new `CustomerProfileConfig` model to store profile configuration data - Added API endpoints for CRUD operations on customer profile configurations - Implemented a configuration menu UI component that allows users to toggle profile tiles - Added feature flag `CUSTOMER_PROFILE_CONFIG_BUTTON` to control the visibility of the configuration feature - Refactored the person profile canvas logic to use the new configuration system - Added ability to save and reset profile configurations ### Activity logs ### Profile with edit button ### Edit menu open ### Profile with unsaved changes ## How did you test this code? - Added backend tests for the new API endpoints - Manually tested the UI by toggling different profile tiles and verifying they appear/disappear correctly - Verified that configurations persist after page refresh - Tested the feature flag functionality to ensure the configuration button only appears when enabled - Verified that default configurations are used when no custom configuration exists ## Publish to changelog? No

title: fix(experiments): don't change browser history on search filter change
body:
## Problem

When typing a search query in the experiments list, each keystroke pushed a new entry to the browser history. This caused the browser back button to remove characters one at a time from the search query instead of navigating to the previous page.

## Changes

Changed URL update behavior in experimentsLogic.ts from replace: false to replace: true for filter changes. This replaces the current history entry instead of pushing new ones, so the back button works as expected.

## How did you test this code?

 Tested locally.

Release notes

Sourced from pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish's releases.

v1.13.0

[!important] 🚨 This release includes fixes for GHSA-vxmw-7h4f-hqxh discovered by @​woodruffw💰. We've also integrated Zizmor to catch similar issues in the future and you should too.

✨ New Stuff

@​woodruffw💰 updated the README to no longer mention the attestations feature being experimental in #347: it's been rather stable for a year already 🎉 He also added more diagnostic output which includes printing out the GitHub Environment claim via #371 and warning about the unsupported reusable workflows configurations #306, when using Trusted Publishing.

[!tip] The official support for reusable workflows is currently blocked on changes to PyPI. To get updates about progress on the action side, you may want to subscribe to #166. At PyCon US 2025 Sprints, @​facutuesca💰, @​miketheman💰, @​woodruffw💰 and I💰 spent several hours IRL brainstorming how to fix this and migrate projects that happen to rely on an obscure corner case with reusable workflows that temporarily allows them to function by accident. The result of that discussion is posted @ pypi/warehouse#11096. Note that this is a volunteer-led effort and there is no ETA. If you need this soon, make your employer sponsor the PSF and maybe they'll be able to hire somebody for this work on Warehouse.

In addition to that, @​konstin💰 sent #378 to pin actions/setup-python to a SHA hash. This makes pypi-publish compatible with new GitHub policies that allow organizations to mandate hash-pinning actions used in workflows.

🛠️ Internal Dependencies

@​webknjaz💰 made a bunch of updates to the action runtime which includes bumping it to Python 3.13 in #331 and updating the dependency tree across the board. pip-with-requires-python is no longer being installed (#332). Some related bumps were contributed by @​woodruffw💰 (#359) and @​kurtmckee💰 sent a contributor-facing PR, bumping the linting configuration via #335.

💪 New Contributors

  • @​kurtmckee made their first contribution in #335
  • @​konstin made their first contribution in #378

🪞 Full Diff: https://github.com/pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish/compare/v1.12.4...v1.13.0

🧔‍♂️ Release Manager: @​webknjaz 🇺🇦

💬 Discuss on Bluesky 🦋, on Mastodon 🐘 and on GitHub.

title: fix(ci): exclude cymbal test fixtures from CodeQL analysis
body:
Test JS chunks in `rust/cymbal/tests/static/` use the ES2022 regex `d` flag (indices/hasIndices) which CodeQL's parser doesn't support yet, causing parse error warnings during analysis.

These are minified test fixtures for source map resolution testing, not production code.
